title: Function Calling
description: What actually happens when a function is called: control jumps to its body, runs, then jumps back to exactly where it left off.
objectives: Understand what happens, step by step, when a function is called
Trace control flow jumping into and back out of a function
Call a function that returns void, with no value to use

sections.text: 1. What Happens When You Call a Function?
Writing a function's name followed by parentheses is a function call. When this line runs, execution doesn't just continue to the next line — it jumps into add's body entirely.

steps: Jump back to right after the call

sections.title: Tracing it
points: add(5, 3) is called — execution jumps into add's body, with a = 5, b = 3
return a + b; computes 8 and sends control back to main(), right where the call happened

sections.text: A function's body can call other functions, just like main() calls add. sumOfSquares calls square twice, once for each argument, before combining the results — building bigger behavior out of smaller, already-working pieces.
4. Calling a Function That Returns Nothing

void means "this function doesn't return a value" — a preview of the next topic, Return Types. Calling greet() just runs its body for what it does (printing), and there's nothing to store the way we stored add's result into a variable.
5. Arguments Are Evaluated Before the Call Happens
Whatever's inside the parentheses at the call site is fully evaluated first — the function only ever sees the final computed values, not the expressions that produced them.

sections.title: Delegating a task and waiting for the result
sections.text: Asking a colleague "can you calculate the total?" is a function call: you hand over the inputs, they go do the work in their own space, and you pick back up exactly where you left off once they hand the answer back. You don't watch every step of their calculation — you just wait for the return value.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the parentheses when calling a function — greet; is not a call, greet(); is.
Expecting to use a value from a void function — there is no returned value to store or print.
Assuming a function call runs "alongside" the rest of the program — execution actually pauses at the call site and waits until the function fully finishes before continuing.
Forgetting arguments are fully evaluated before the call — add(x++, x) can be confusing exactly because both arguments are computed before add even starts running.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What happens when a function is called?
questions.answer: Execution jumps from the call site into the function's body, runs its statements, and then jumps back to right after the call once the function finishes (via return or reaching the end).

questions.question: Can a function call another function?
questions.answer: Yes — a function's body can call any other function, allowing bigger behavior to be built from smaller, already-defined functions.

questions.question: What does it mean to call a void function?
questions.answer: It runs the function's body for its effect (like printing something), but there is no return value to store or use afterward.

questions.question: Are a function's arguments evaluated before or during the call?
questions.answer: Before — any expressions passed as arguments are fully computed first, and the function only receives their final values.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Calling a function — writing its name with parentheses — transfers control from the call site into the function's body. The function runs its statements, and if it returns a value, execution jumps back to exactly where the call happened, with that value available to use. Functions can call other functions, building larger behavior from smaller pieces, and a void function can be called purely for its effect, with no value returned. Arguments inside the call's parentheses are fully evaluated before the function itself starts running.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Function Calling
sections.text: Calling a function = delegate the task, pause, wait for the result, then continue exactly where you left off.
